course_description: Basic statistical concepts & techniques such as descriptive statistics, probability concepts, theoretical distributions, inferential statistics (confidence intervals & hypothesis testing) are applied in business & economics.

requirements.prerequisites: St Lucia: Maths B or Maths C or MATH1040 Ipswich: Maths A or Maths B or Maths C
requirements.incompatible: EC134 or EC135 or CHEE3010 or MINE3214 or STAT1201 or 2201 or (MS101+(102 or 112) or 103 or 113 or 240 or (PSYC1040+2010) or (PY103+270 or 272)
requirements.assumed_background: Algebra from high school Maths B.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Additional Timetable Information
Students are required to sign-on for a lecture and a tutorial group via mySI-net. For tutorials, procedures are strict. After mySI-net no longer allows you to change groups yourself (teaching week 2), you may only change groups by submitting a request in writing and receiving approval from the Course Administrator. Further details regarding sign-on requirements can be found on the course website on Blackboard under " Course Information ".

Tutorials and Lectures commence in Teaching Week 1.

PASS (Peer Assisted Study Sessions) commences in Teaching Week 3.

Students should refer to the timetable on mySI-net prior to the commencement of classes to ensure that they have the most up to date information, as from time to time late room changes may occur.

Please see the Learning Activities section of this Course Profile (Section 4.1) for the timetabling implications of public holidays .

Next 2. Aims
course_aims:
ECON1310 is an introductory course in quantitative analysis for business and economics.
The course aims to introduce students
* to a range of statistical techniques and applications that would be useful in economic and business environments.
* to using Excel to perform statistical computations and interpret the results.
* to considering the limitations of the techniques as well as their appropriate use.
* to inferential statistics and regression which will provide a solid foundation for the follow-on subject ECON1320 (Quantitative and Business Analysis B) and Econometrics.

learning_outcomes.description: Apply descriptive statistical concepts in economic and business situations.
Use basic probability concepts and recognize how they form the foundation of statistical inference.
Construct and apply a number of probability distributions commonly used in economic and business analysis.
Apply methods of statistical inference and demonstrate an ability to draw appropriate conclusions from sample data.
Use and interpret simple linear regression analysis in economics and business.

learning_activities.topic: Wed Lect 1: Descriptive Statistics 1 : Statistical concepts and definitions; data types, sources, levels; descriptive & inferential statistics; sampling methods and errors. Learning Objectives: 1 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 1, Ch 7.1)
Wed Maths Workshop

Wed Lect 2: Descriptive Statistics 2 : measures of central tendency, variation and shape for ungrouped data; box and whisker plot; linear combination of random variables; coefficient of correlation. Learning Objectives: 1 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 3.1, 3.2, 3.4, 3.5)
Wed Tutorial 1: Descriptive Statistics 1 : tutorial on work covered in Lecture 1. Learning Objectives: 1 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 1.2-1.4, 7.1)
Wed Lect 3: Probability 1 : basic probability concepts, conditional probability. Learning Objectives: 2 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 4.1-4.7)
Wed Tutorial 2: Descriptive Statistics 2 : tutorial on work covered in Lecture 2. Learning Objectives: 1 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 3.1, 3.2, 3.4, 3.5)
Wed Lect 4: Probability 2 : discrete probability distributions, binomial probability. Learning Objectives: 2, 3 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 5.1-5.3)
Wed Tutorial 3: Probability 1 : tutorial on work covered in Lecture 3. Learning Objectives: 2 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 4.1-4.7)

Wed Lect 5: Normal Distribution : understanding the basis of the normal distribution, interpretting the Standardised Normal Distribution table, computing Z - scores and finding probabilities. Learning Objectives: 3 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 6.2)
Wed Tutorial 4: Probability 2 : tutorial on work covered in Lecture 4. Learning Objectives: 2, 3 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 5.1-5.3)
Wed Lect 6: Sampling Distributions : sampling distributions of the mean and of the proportion. Learning Objectives: 3, 4 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 7.2 - 7.3)
Wed Tutorial 5: Normal Distribution : tutorial on work covered in Lecture 5. Learning Objectives: 3 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 6.2)

Wed Lect 7: Confidence Intervals 1 : confidence interval estimate for the mean using both Z and t distributions; confidence interval for proportion. Learning Objectives: 4 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 8.1-8.3)
Wed Tutorial 6: Sampling Distributions : tutorial on work covered in Lecture 6. Learning Objectives: 3, 4 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 7.2 - 7.3)

Wed Lect 8: Confidence Intervals 2 : sample size determination for the mean and proportion; confidence interval for the difference between two means. Learning Objectives: 4 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 8.5, 10.2 pp 372-373, 377-378)

Wed Tutorial 7: Confidence Intervals 1 : tutorial on work covered in Lecture 7 NB. Friday 22nd April - Good Friday Public Holiday. Students signed-on to a Friday tutorial or PASS session should attend another class this week.
Mon - Sun Mid-Semester Break : No lectures this week.
Mon - Sun Mid-Semester Break : No tutorials or PASS classes this week.

Wed Lect 9: Hypothesis Testing 1 : hypothesis testing methodology; one and two-tailed tests on the mean using critical value approach (Z and t); types of errors possible. Learning Objectives: 4 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 9.1-9.3, 9.6)

Wed Tutorial 8: Confidence Intervals 2 : tutorial on work covered in Lecture 8. NB. Monday 2 May - Labour Day Public Holiday. Students signed-on to a Monday tutorial or PASS session should attend another class this week.

Wed Lect 10: Hypothesis Testing 2 : p-value approach to hypothesis testing; test for proportion; pooled variance test for difference between two means. Learning Objectives: 4 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 9.3, 9.4 10.2)
Wed Tutorial 9: Hypothesis Testing 1 : tutorial on work covered in Lecture 9. Learning Objectives: 4 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 9.1-9.3, 9.6)

Wed Lect 11: Simple Linear Regression 1 : SLR model; equation estimation using Excel; coefficient of determination; standard error of the estimate; confidence interval for the slope. Learning Objectives: 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 13.1-13.2, 13.4-13.5)
Wed Tutorial 10: Hypothesis Testing 2 : tutorial on work covered in Lecture 10. Learning Objectives: 4 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (9.3, 9.4, 10.2)
Wed Lect 12: Simple Linear Regression 2 : assumptions; residual analysis; hypothesis test on slope. Learning Objectives: 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (13.3, 13.6)
Wed Tutorial 11: Simple Linear Regression 1 : tutorial on work covered in Lecture 11. Learning Objectives: 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 13.1, 13.2, 13.4, 13.5)
Wed Lect 13: Revision Learning Objectives: 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 6 - 10, 13)
Wed Tutorial 12: Simple Linear Regression 2 : tutorial on work covered in Lecture 12. Learning Objectives: 5 Readings/Ref: Prescribed text (Ch 13.3, 13.6)

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the Universitys assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity
It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism
The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Academic Integrity and Plagiarism policy ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25128 ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

Medical grounds: Applications for extension on medical grounds shall be made by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and supporting documentation at the location outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course concerned.

Exceptional circumstances: Applications for extension on the grounds of exceptional circumstances shall be made to the relevant Course Coordinator/Program Director by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and a personal statement outlining the grounds for the application at the location and by the due date outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course in which this application is made. If the exceptional circumstances are such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have complied with these conditions, a case should be made as to why these conditions could not be met.

Outcome of application: Students will be advised of the outcome of their application via their student email.

Feedback on Assessment
Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25109 )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

Feedback in this Course

Feedback is obtained from the CML quizzes, and via Consultation with tutors and lecturer. Viewing sessions will be held for the midsemester and final exams - further details will be bulk-emailed to student email addresses at the relevant time.

School of Economics
Assessment Guidelines

School of Economics Assessment Guidelines

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses
Students on a placement course also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25120 ) for detailed information.

Working with Children
Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25004 ) to find out how to apply for a blue card.

Students with a Disability
Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Adviser at Student Support Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25123 ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25111

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety
Undergraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25055 ) and Postgraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25057 ) should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory.

Other Course Guidelines

In the School of Economics there is only one brand and model of calculator allowed into examinations, and it is CASIO FX 82 (with any letters) . Refer to Section 3.4 for further details.

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
ECON1310 is an introductory course in quantitative analysis for business and economics. The course covers a variety of techniques applicable to the presentation, interpretation and use of data. The main emphasis is inferential statistics with Estimation and Hypothesis Testing techniques being an important part of the course. Inferential statistics is continued in the simple linear regression topic. There is an emphasis on the use of Excel for analysis and presentation.

This course is widely regarded as challenging by many students. One reason is that the work is totally cumulative. This means that it is exceptionally difficult to catch up if a student gets behind in the work. Success in the subject depends on keeping up-to-date. With this in mind, each topic is assessed as it is completed via a computer managed quiz.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

In response to student requests, additional practice questions and solutions will be provided on Blackboard throughout the semester.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Undergrad Pass) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A1 . A comprehensive and well-founded knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A4 . An understanding of how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
C1 . The ability to work and learn independently .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C4 . The ability to identify problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
D1 . The ability to define and analyse problems .
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
ECON1310 is a #2 unit course. UQ's expectation is that a #2 unit course requires a time allocation of, on average, 10 hours per week. Lectures, tutorials and PASS only account for 4.5 hours per week. Therefore, if you are attending all of these contact sessions, it is expected that you will be spending a further 5.5 hours per week in self-directed learning. This could include attending consultation sessions, reading the textbook, attempting practice questions at the end of each textbook chapter, etc. Please give serious consideration as to whether you are willing and able to make this time commitment before enrolling. Experience shows that students who do not put in this basic time allocation tend to struggle.

This ECON1310 offering is an internal-mode course. That is, UQ offers lectures and tutorial classes and expects that you will be attending them. We also strongly recommend that you attend PASS classes but this is your choice. There is good reason to "show up". We've analysed the link between attendance and assessment performance. The relationship is positive and statistically significant.

ECON1310 is a large course. Despite this, don't be shy to seek out one-on-one help from teaching staff during consultation sessions. They are there to help. Note however, that staff on consultation will expect that you have been performing your side of the bargain, i.e., dedicating 10 hours per week to ECON1310 - attending lectures/tutorials, reading your textbook, etcetera. If it becomes obvious that you have not, you'll likely be asked to return when you have done so.

Ensure you attain the marks necessary to achieve the grade to which you aspire. We are aware that some students aspire to a 6 or 7, whilst others aspire to a 4. All such aspirations are fine with us. Our job is to clearly specify the grade cut-offs, clearly state the marking criteria and then mark assessment according to this criteria. In a very large course such as ECON1310, it is inevitable that some students will end up close (i.e. within 0.5%) of a grade cut-off. This is not unusual or unfair. You control your own destiny - ensure you are on the right side of a grade cut-off. Students can, and do, fail ECON1310 by 0.5%. Missing out on a grade cut-off by 0.5% is not grounds for a re-mark or any special consideration. Assessment results will only be revisited if a marking error has occurred (e.g. assessment has not been graded according to the stated marking criteria), or if an administrative error has occurred (e.g. results for individual assessment pieces have not been added up correctly). If you feel that either of these have occurred, contact us immediately. After each assessable item, students will be given the opportunity to view their assessment (exam-viewing session times and locations will be posted on Blackboard) and so satisfy themselves that a marking or administrative error has not occurred.

The formal process of querying a course result (requesting a remark on academic grounds) is set out in 3.30.1 Assessment, Section 7 of the Handbook of University Policies and Procedures ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/?page=25109 ).

Make sure you check your student email regularly . All important correspondence will be communicated to students, throughout the semester by email and via the Course Website on Blackboard.

Lectures

You are required to sign on and attend one, two hour lecture each week (sign-on will be available on mySI-net until the end of Teaching Week 2). If there are circumstances that prevent you from attending a lecture, it is your responsibility to find out what was covered.

Lectures build on each other. This means that skipping classes can quickly become detrimental to your learning and progress in the course.

Lecture notes will posted on Blackboard. These slides will only be a summary of what will be covered in the lecture. Downloading and printing these from Blackboard should not be seen as a substitute for lecture attendance. For example, a lecture slide may simply pose a question with the answer left to be discussed during the lecture.

St Lucia lectures will be recorded using UQ's lecture recording software, Lectopia, and subsequently made available for download on Blackboard. However, again, we emphasise that this is an internal-mode course, not distance education. Lectopia only offers a visual of the Powerpoint / Visualiser presentation and an audio overlay of the lecturer's microphone. It does not capture any visual of the lecture presentation itself or any verbal interaction between the lecturer and students, which often can be extensive. Accordingly, while it can be viewed as a useful supplementary material, it cannot be seen as a viable substitute for lecture attendance and should only be used for revision purposes.

Note that a lecture is not intended to be a summary of a textbook chapter. Rather, a lecture seeks to add value in some way. For example, lectures might include the application of theory, covered in the textbook, to an unusual real-world situation. Lectures also help guide you on what parts of the textbook are the most relevant for assessment or other purposes. Extensions of textbook material made in lectures are assessable unless otherwise indicated.

Tutorials

You are expected to sign-on and attend one two-hour tutorial per week for ECON1310. The first tutorials are being held in WEEK 1 and these will be assessing your level of maths ability, and giving you practice in the areas you will need for the ECON1310 content.

From week 2, all of the tutorials in any given week cover the same work and are based on material covered in the previous Teaching Week's lecture. Tutorial registration is available via mySI-net, until Thursday 10 March. Please refer to the ECON1310 Course Website on Blackboard for further details regarding tutorial sign-on requirements. A record of tutorial attendance will be kept and may be used in advising on matters involving withdrawal. As far as possible (spaces permitting), you will be allowed flexibility to change your signed-on tutorial time during the first two weeks on mySI-net. After this you must remain in the group that you signed on to. Attendance is monitored. If you need to change tutorial groups after sign-on has closed (due to an extenuating reason), you must put your request in writing to Ms Lauren Summerson for approval. Tutorials are capped at 20 students per class to ensure an effective learning environment. For this reason, where overcrowding occurs preference will always be given to students who have signed on. The tutorials play a very important role in this course. They are a student's main opportunity to seek individual assistance and further explanation from teaching staff regarding any areas of the subject. Exercises for the tutorials will be posted on the course website on Blackboard, throughout the semester. Most tutorials work through a series of pre-set questions. You should work through the material before coming to the tutorial otherwise what you get out of the class will be limited. Do not expect your tutor to simply provide you with the answers. This is not their job. The tutor will direct questions to students during the class and active participation is encouraged.

Consultation

Consultation is your chance to speak directly with the teaching staff, one on one. A consultation timetable listing the consultation times / locations of teaching staff will be posted on the Course Website on Blackboard under "Course Documents". There is no need to make an appointment for consultation and you may attend any teaching staff's consultation session. Note that consultation sessions may be busy prior to an exam or assessment time.

Peer Assisted Study Sessions (PASS)

PASS offers students a fun and interactive way to review the important material covered in each lecture, practice past exam questions and deepen their understanding of key concepts. PASS sessions are run by past students who've done well in ECON1310 and they're an excellent opportunity to get the student's perspective on the course - what to focus on in revision, useful study techniques and advice for the exam. Sessions are informal - all questions and queries are welcomed and PASS leaders are happy to tailor classes to the interests of the group. It's a great way for you to participate in group learning, as well as to make some more friends at University. PASS sign-on will be available as "contact" sign-on on mySI-net. Note that spaces are limited and preference is always given to those students who are officially signed-on to a session. PASS does not replace tutorials, but aims to supplement them. Students who attend both lectures and tutorials will gain the maximum benefit from PASS. For more information on class times, the PASS work booklet etc, please head to the PASS link on the ECON1310 Blackboard page. PASS commences in Teaching Week 3 .

CML Quizzes

These are a learning tool as well as an assessment. Students appropriately should complete the lecture revision and tutorial work prior to attempting each quiz. Students can learn from mistakes made by reworking questions marked wrong to obtain the correct answer. The regular completion of these assessment tasks will assist students in consolidating the information learned in lectures and tutorials, as well as give valuable feedback on those areas which require more work. Students will be given an optional second attempt at the quizzes in order to improve a mark and better learn the topic - your best score from the two attempts for each quiz will contribute towards your aggregate CML result. Further information regarding CML quizzes can be found in the Assessment section of this Course Profile, and on the ECON1310 Course Website on Blackboard.

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

Negligible knowledge and comprehension of statistical concepts; unable to solve applied numerical problems; provides wrong explanations to theory-based questions. Semester mark between 0% and 19.99%.

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

Little evidence of understanding of statistical concepts; uses incorrect methods in solving applied numerical problems; provides poor explanations to theory-based questions. Semester mark between 20% and 44.99%.

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

Semester mark between 45% and 49.99%.

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Demonstrates a basic understanding of statistical concepts; uses partly correct methods in solving applied numerical problems with some errors; provides fair explanations to theory-based questions. Semester mark between 50% and 64.99%.

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Demonstrates a fair understanding of statistical concepts; is able to use correct methods in solving applied numerical problems with some errors; provides satisfactory explanations to theory-based questions. Semester mark between 65% and 74.99%.

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

Demonstrates a good understanding of statistical concepts; is able to use correct methods in solving applied numerical problems with minor errors or omissions; provides good explanations to theory-based questions. Semester marks between 75% and 84.99%.

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

Demonstrates an excellent mastery of statistical concepts; is able to use correct methods in solving applied numerical problems with minimal or no errors; provides excellent explanations to theory based questions. Semester mark of 85% +.
late_submission: No late submission is possible. CML Access is blocked after the due date and time.
other_assessment_information:
CML Quizzes

Step by step Instructions for the CML quizzes will be posted on the ECON1310 Course Website on Blackboard.

A students best five (out of six) CML marks will be used in calculating the aggregate mark which contributes 20% towards the final grade for the semester.

An optional second attempt is allowed for all except the final quiz (SLR). A student's best score from the two attempts for each quiz will contribute towards their aggregate CML result.

SPECIAL EXAMINATIONS

If you are unable to attend a scheduled exam (e.g. due to illness or other unavoidable circumstances which can be evidenced - e.g. medical certificate), you may be eligible to sit a special examination at a later time.

The special exam application form can be downloaded from here:

http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor//forms/exams/SpecAppForm.pdf

Information on grounds for applying for special examinations can be viewed here:

http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/grounds-for-applying-for-special-examinations

General information about special examinations can be found on the web at:

http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/special-examinations Please read this information very carefully.

Application Procedure:

Note that the midsemester exam is a SCHOOL exam, and the final exam is a CENTRAL EXAM .

Mid-Semester Examination - s ubmit your application to Lauren Summerson with appropriate documentation attached (e.g. medical certificate) no later than 5 calendar days after the original exam was held . Do NOT submit applications to the BEL Faculty Collaborative Learning Centre or the Student Centre.

End of Semester Examination - submit your application with appropriate documentation to the Student Centre, Level 1, J.D. Story Building no later than 5 calendar days after the original exam was held .

An application made on medical grounds must be accompanied by a medical certificate signed by a medical practitioner or registered nurse no later than two (2) business days after the examination and stating that the student was unfit to sit the examination on the relevant day.

An application made by a student who is incapacitated during an examination must be accompanied by a medical certificate signed by a medical practitioner or registered nurse within one (1) business day of the examination . The student must inform the examination supervisor of their intention to apply for a special examination and sign a Disregard Performance declaration prior to leaving the examination.

An application made on non-medical grounds (i.e. based on extenuating or unavoidable circumstances) must be supported by corroborating information.

Note: " Special examinations are not granted where the executive dean or head of school was not satisfied that the student took reasonable measures (steps) to avoid the circumstance that contributed to the student missing or failing to complete the examination. These include holiday arrangements (including overseas travel), misreading an examination timetable, social and leisure events including sporting and cultural committments other than at state, national or international representative level."

SUPPLEMENTARY EXAMINATIONS

If you fail a course (generally with a grade of 3), you may be permitted a "second chance" to pass the course by being granted a supplementary examination. Supplementary examinations will be granted only where the General Award Rules and your Program Rules allow. Official rules can be found under the Information for current students or Program requirements links for each program on the Programs and Couses website.

Please check the rules for your program and with your faculty to determine eligibility.

Applications must be lodged online via mySI-net, within 5 calendar days of the official release of results . For further information and how to apply please see http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/supplementary-assessment-2896 .

Important Information regarding the ECON1310 Supplementary Examination:

Supplementary Examinations are weighted at 100% (i.e. progressive assessment does not count) and therefore ALL modules and topics are assessable.

Results for Supplementary Examinations cannot be graded higher than a four (4).

Once approved, applications cannot be rescinded.

IMPORTANT - Final Examinations

Students are advised to take the Special and Supplementary Exam period into account when making post-examination plans (20-22 July).

If you have been granted a Special or Supplementary examination, you are expected to be available to sit your examination/s on any day or days of the specified period. Timetables will be published several days prior to the commencement of the Supplementary/Special examination period and may be accessed through my.UQ and mySI-net .
